1               Feudalism to Modernity

The transition from Feudalism to Modernity marked a structural transformation from an agrarian, hierarchical, and Church-influenced
feudal order to an urban, commercial, and rational society. Driven by the decline of feudalism, the growth of trade and towns, and
the Renaissance, it laid the foundations for capitalism, modern nation-states, and later industrialisation.
1.1. Renaissance
The Renaissance (14th-16th centuries) was a cultural and intellectual movement that revived classical Greek and Roman learning,
leading to major advances in art, literature, science, and exploration. Originating in Italy, it was driven by the decline of feudalism,
trade and urban growth, and patronage. It marked the transition from a medieval, religion-dominated worldview to one centred
on humanism and secular inquiry, before spreading across Europe and shaping the modern world.

The Renaissance marked a profound transformation in European thought, sparking advances in art, science, and humanism
that bridged the Middle Ages and modern era. As Leonardo da Vinci famously said, "Learning never exhausts the mind."
This spirit of curiosity and intellectual revival defined the era. Its enduring legacy continues to shape global culture, intellectual
inquiry, and the way humanity understands itself and the world.

1.2. Industrial Revolution in England
The Industrial Revolution, which began in England in the late eighteenth century, marked a transformative shift from
traditional hand production to mechanised manufacturing. This era profoundly altered the nation’s economy and society,
laying the foundations of modern industrial society.
                                                UPSC Prelims PYQs
     1.    How far was the Industrial Revolution in England responsible for the decline of
           handicrafts and cottage industries in India? (2024)
     2.    Why did the Industrial Revolution first occur in England? Discuss the quality of life of
           the people there during the industrialization. How does it compare with that in India at
           Present? (2015)

The Industrial Revolution in England was a defining era that transformed the nation’s economy, society, and global influence,
ushering in rapid industrialisation and technological progress. However, as historians aptly stated, “Britain’s Industrial
Revolution was actually premised upon the deindustrialisation of India", highlighting how this progress was deeply
intertwined with the decline of traditional industries and economic exploitation in colonies like India.

2            American Revolution

The American Revolution (1763-1783) was a transformative struggle in which the Thirteen Colonies fought against British rule,
ultimately establishing the United States as an independent nation founded on the principles of liberty and self-government. As
Thomas Jefferson asserted in A Summary View of the Rights of British America (1774), "Still less let it be proposed that our
properties within our own territories shall be taxed or regulated by any power on earth but our own," reflecting the colonial
demand for self-taxation and representative government. Unlike the more socially radical French Revolution, the American
Revolution primarily sought political independence, pioneering a written constitution and the establishment of a constitutional
republic based on representative government.


                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    Explain how the foundations of the modern world were laid by the American and French
           revolutions. (2019)
     2.    The American Revolution was an economic revolt against mercantilism. Substantiate.
           (2013).


2.1. Causes of American Revolution
       Cause                                                        Description
 Mercantilist           Britain pursued mercantilism, treating colonies as sources of raw materials and captive markets.
 Exploitation           After the Seven Years' War, it imposed measures like the Stamp Act, Sugar Act, and Townshend Acts,
                        generating widespread economic discontent among colonists.
 Lack of Political      The colonists were denied representation in the British Parliament yet were subjected to taxation,
 Representation         leading to the powerful assertion: “No taxation without representation.”
 End of Salutary        The earlier policy of salutary neglect had allowed self-governing institutions to evolve. Its abrupt end
 Neglect                after 1763 was perceived as an attack on established colonial autonomy.
 Enlightenment          Thinkers like John Locke, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, and Montesquieu inspired ideas of natural rights,
 Ideas (Ideological     popular sovereignty, and limited government, providing moral justification for resistance.
 Foundation)
 Organised              Groups such as the Sons of Liberty and Committees of Correspondence transformed scattered
 Resistance             protests into a coordinated movement, promoting boycotts, communication, and unity among
                        colonies.
 Role of                Influential figures like Thomas Paine, Benjamin Franklin, and Patrick Henry articulated colonial
 Intellectuals          grievances and mobilised public opinion in favour of independence.
 Proclamation of        The Proclamation of 1763 curtailed westward expansion, restricting economic prospects and
 1763                   intensifying colonial resentment.
 Grown Colonial         Expanding trade networks and economic activity fostered self-reliance, encouraging greater economic
 Confidence             self-confidence and aspirations for autonomy
 Immediate              Incidents such as the Boston Massacre and Boston Tea Party escalated tensions, radicalised public
 Triggers               sentiment, and made reconciliation increasingly unlikely.
 Coercive Acts          The Coercive Acts imposed stringent punitive measures, uniting the colonies and acting as the
                        immediate catalyst for open rebellion.

                                                Impacts on the World

       Impact                                                       Description
Spread of Democracy       The Revolution showed that people can create their own government based on popular
& Republicanism           sovereignty (power of the people). It inspired the French Revolution and other democratic
                          movements.

Movements'                the Caribbean, and later India to fight for independence.

Movements                 women’s rights, and civil rights across the world.

                          opportunity.

Impacts of the American Revolution on India

       Impact                                                       Description
Shift in British          The loss of the American colonies (1783) made India the centrepiece of the British Empire,
Imperial Focus            increasing its strategic and economic importance.
Expansion of British      Britain accelerated its territorial expansion through successive wars against Mysore, the
Territorial Control       Marathas, and the Sikhs, leading to the establishment of British political paramountcy in

Administrative            To strengthen control over its most valuable colony, Parliament increased oversight of the East

2.4. Key Factors Behind the Colonists' Victory
        Reason                                                       Description
Unified Purpose            The American colonists (Patriots) were united by a clear goal of independence from Britain –
                           which sustained their resistance despite internal divisions and the presence of Loyalists.
International Aid          The colonists received decisive external support, especially from France, along with Spain and
                           the Dutch Republic, in the form of money, troops, weapons, and naval assistance.
Effective Colonial         The colonial forces were led effectively by George Washington, who maintained discipline,
Leadership                 unity, and long-term strategy within the Continental Army.
Strong Colonial Public     The colonial population actively backed the revolution, providing supplies, intelligence, and
Support                    manpower through militias.
Use of Local Geography     The colonists used their familiarity with local terrain to adopt flexible and defensive tactics,
by Colonists               which made it difficult for British forces to control and defeat them.
Exploitation of British    The colonists benefited from and took advantage of British mistakes, including underestimation of
Weaknesses                 colonial strength, long supply lines, and slow communication across the Atlantic.
Revolutionary Ideas        Colonial writers like Thomas Paine spread ideas of liberty and independence, which mobilised
Mobilised Colonists        colonial society and strengthened their resolve.
Decisive Colonial          The final success of the colonists was sealed at the Battle of Yorktown (1781), where British
Victory                    forces surrendered, effectively ending major military operations.


The American Revolution established the United States as one of the earliest modern constitutional republics grounded in the
principles of liberty, representative government, and constitutionalism, while inspiring democratic and anti-colonial movements
across the world. Its success marked a turning point in world history, proving that Enlightenment ideals could be achieved in practice
and transforming political, social, and economic systems globally. As Thomas Jefferson famously declared in the Declaration of
Independence, "We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal."

3            French Revolution

The French Revolution (1789-1799) was a period of intense upheaval in France marked by the overthrow of the monarchy, radical
social change, and mass mobilisation. Sparked by economic hardship, social inequality, and enlightenment ideas, it dramatically
transformed French society and governance.
                                               UPSC Mains PYQs
     1.    The French Revolution has enduring relevance to the contemporary world. Explain. (2025)
     2.    Explain how the foundations of the modern world were laid by the American and French
           revolutions. (2019)


                                    The Political sytstem under the Constitution of 1791


3.1. Causes of French Revolution
      Aspect                                                           Description
Economic                   The Third Estate (peasants, urban labourers, bourgeoisie) bore heavy taxation, while the First
Inequality                 (clergy) and Second (nobility) enjoyed tax exemptions, creating deep resentment.
Financial Crisis           Costly wars such as the Seven Years' War and American Revolution, along with an inefficient
                           taxation system and failure of fiscal reforms, pushed France into severe debt; extravagant
                           spending aggravated the crisis.
Social Injustice           The Third Estate suffered from feudal dues, tithes, limited social mobility, and political under-
                           representation, while elites retained hereditary privileges.
Urban Discontent           Unemployment and rising bread prices (due to poor harvests, 1788-89) led to food shortages,
                           inflation, urban unrest, and riots.
Enlightenment              Enlightenment ideas, particularly Jean-Jacques Rousseau's concept of popular sovereignty and
Influence                  Voltaire's advocacy of freedom, reason, and religious tolerance, challenged absolute monarchy,
                           privilege, and social inequality.
American                   The American Revolution provided ideological inspiration and also worsened financial strain;
Revolution’s Impact        figures like Marquis de Lafayette spread revolutionary ideas.
Weak Monarchical           Louis XVI displayed indecisiveness, administrative failure, and inability to implement reforms,
Leadership                 weakening royal authority.
Marie Antoinette’s         Marie Antoinette symbolised royal extravagance, foreign influence, and insensitivity, fuelling
Unpopularity               public anger.

3.2. Impacts of the French Revolution
     Aspect                                                 Description & Significance
Political Changes        Abolished absolute monarchy and introduced republican and constitutional principles based on
                         popular sovereignty (power rests with the people). Promoted ideas of democracy, constitutionalism,
                         and citizenship, influencing political systems across Europe.
Economic Impact          Ended feudal dues and unequal taxation, promoting fair taxation and reducing aristocratic
                         privileges. Facilitated transfer of Church and aristocratic land redistribution to peasants (though not
                         uniform). However, it also caused short-term instability, inflation, and food shortages.
Social Changes           Dismantled the feudal hierarchy by abolishing the privileges of the nobility and clergy,
                         promoted legal equality, secular citizenship, and expanded civil rights for men. It also fostered
                         nationalism, encouraging people to identify themselves as citizens of a nation rather than
                         subjects of a king. Although women played an active role (e.g., the March to Versailles), they
                         remained politically excluded.
International            Spread ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity across Europe and Latin America. Inspired
Impact                   nationalist and anti-colonial movements and influenced modern Constitutions, including that of
                         India.
Military Strategies      Introduced Levée en masse (mass conscription), transforming warfare from professional armies to
                         citizen armies. Strengthened nationalism and influenced modern military organisation.
Influence on Arts        The Revolution influenced artistic and literary movements, including Romanticism and nationalist
and Culture              themes. Emphasised liberty, civic virtue, and the role of citizens in shaping society.

3.3. Impact of French Revolution on larger European Continent
    Impact                                                          Description
Spread of             The Revolution popularised the ideals of Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity, inspiring demands for
Liberalism            constitutional government, civil liberties, rule of law, and representative institutions across Europe.
Rise of               It promoted the principle of popular sovereignty, asserting that sovereignty belongs to the nation rather
Nationalism           than the monarch. This inspired nationalist movements in Italy, Germany, Poland, Greece, Hungary,
                      and Belgium, paving the way for later unification and independence movements.


 Decline of           Revolutionary and Napoleonic reforms abolished feudal privileges, serfdom, guild restrictions, and
 Feudalism            aristocratic privileges in many French-controlled territories, accelerating Europe's transition to a
                      modern social and economic order.
 Legal and            The Napoleonic Code introduced equality before the law, protection of property rights, secular
 Administrative       administration, merit-based appointments, and centralised governance, laying the foundation of the
 Reforms              modern state.
 Challenge to         The Revolution proved that absolute monarchies could be overthrown through popular movements,
 Absolute             weakening dynastic legitimacy and encouraging constitutional reforms across Europe.
 Monarchy
 Conservative         European monarchies responded by forming coalitions against Revolutionary France. After Napoleon's
 Reaction             defeat, the Congress of Vienna (1815) and the Concert of Europe sought to restore monarchy,
                      legitimacy, and the balance of power.
 Wave of Liberal      Revolutionary ideals inspired the Revolutions of 1830 and 1848, in which liberals and nationalists
 and Nationalist      demanded constitutional rule, political representation, and national unity across Europe.
 Revolutions

3.4. Relevance of French Revolution in Contemporary World
The French Revolution was a transformative turning point in world history that dismantled the foundations of feudalism and
absolute monarchy and ushered in the ideals of Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity, along with popular sovereignty. Far
from being a confined historical event, these principles evolved into the foundation of modern democratic governance,
constitutionalism, human rights, secularism, and social solidarity, making the Revolution's legacy deeply relevant in shaping
contemporary political and social systems across the world.

       Aspect                                 Explanation                                           Examples
 Democracy &             The French Revolution rejected absolute monarchy and        Electoral democracies such as India and
 Popular                 established that sovereignty lies with the people,          France, where governments derive

The French Revolution marked the transition from feudal absolutism to modern democracy, establishing ideals of liberty, equality,
and popular sovereignty. It laid the foundation for constitutional governance, human rights, and secularism across the world.
At the same time, its excesses highlight the need to balance freedom with stability in governance.


3.5. Napoleon Bonaparte in French Revolution
Napoleon Bonaparte emerged as one of the most influential figures of the French Revolution, rising to power amid the
political instability that followed the Reign of Terror and the failure of the Directory. He consolidated many revolutionary
ideals through far-reaching legal, administrative, economic, and religious reforms, while simultaneously establishing an
authoritarian empire. Through the Napoleonic Wars, he also spread the principles of the Revolution across Europe, leaving
a lasting impact on modern governance and nationalism.

Reasons for the Rise of Napoleon Bonaparte

 Reputation            France's most celebrated military leader.

 Public Demand         After a decade of revolution, violence, and inflation, people preferred stability, security, and efficient
 for Order             administration over political experimentation.

 Departure from Revolutionary          By crowning himself Emperor (1804), imposing censorship, and concentrating power,
 Democracy                             Napoleon replaced the revolutionary republic with an authoritarian empire.
 Enduring Legacy                       His legal, administrative, and educational reforms outlasted his rule and influenced
                                       governance and legal systems across Europe and beyond.

                     – the United States.                                   republic, followed by instability and eventual rise of
                                                                            Napoleon Bonaparte.

                     Enlightenment thought.                                 influenced by the success of the American
                                                                            Revolution.

 On Slavery          Slavery continued after independence and was           Slavery was abolished in 1794, reintroduced by

The French Revolution fundamentally reshaped the political and social landscape of not only France but the entire world, championing
the ideals of "Liberty, Equality, Fraternity". Its enduring legacy continues to inspire struggles for democracy, justice, and human
rights across the globe. As Maximilien Robespierre once declared, "The secret of freedom lies in educating people, whereas
the secret of tyranny is in keeping them ignorant." This powerful quote reflects the Revolution’s core belief in empowering citizens
through knowledge and equality.

4               Unification of Italy
                        and Germany
The unification of Italy and Germany in the 19th century marked a turning point in European history. Driven by nationalism,
diplomacy, and strategic wars, fragmented states were consolidated into unified nations. Italy's unification was led by figures like
Cavour and Garibaldi, while Germany’s was orchestrated by Otto von Bismarck through his policy of “blood and iron".

4.1. Unification of Italy
The Unification of Italy, or Risorgimento, was a 19th-century political and social movement that consolidated the fragmented
states of the Italian Peninsula into the Kingdom of Italy. Beginning in the early nineteenth century and largely completed
with the annexation of Rome in 1870 and its declaration as capital in 1871, this process transformed Italy from a patchwork
of states into a unified nation-state.


                                                Italian States Before Unification


                                              Italy After Unification

Revolutionary     Revolutionary groups like the Carbonari and Young Italy campaigned for independence and national
Societies         unification.
Leadership of     Leaders such as Mazzini, Cavour, and Garibaldi were instrumental in advancing the cause of Italian
Key Figures       unification.
Impact of         Napoleon’s reorganisation of Italian territories, liberal reforms, and spread of nationalist ideas
Napoleonic Rule   weakened old feudal structures and laid foundations for unification.
Economic          Political division created trade barriers and economic inefficiency. The bourgeoisie (middle class)
Factors           supported unification for a common market and to promote economic growth.

Nationalism       determination encouraged people to demand a nation-state.

The unification of Italy, culminating with the annexation of Rome in 1870, transformed a divided peninsula into a centralised
nation-state and strengthened the development of Italian national consciousness. As Giuseppe Mazzini aptly stated, “Ideas grow
quickly when watered by the blood of martyrs,” reflecting how the sacrifices of countless patriots inspired not only Italian unity
but also nationalist movements across Europe.
4.2. Unification of Germany
The Unification of Germany was a 19th-century process that brought together numerous independent German states into a single
nation under Prussian leadership , excluding Austria. Orchestrated primarily by Otto von Bismarck through a combination of
diplomacy and strategic wars, this movement culminated in the proclamation of the German Empire in 1871.

The American Civil War concluded with the Union's victory and the abolition of slavery, fundamentally reshaping the nation’s
identity and reshaping debates over union, citizenship, and slavery. As President Abraham Lincoln once said, “A house divided
against itself cannot stand.” However, the war’s end also marked the beginning of a long and challenging process of
Reconstruction, as the United States grappled with the enduring legacies of division and inequality.

6               Late Industrialization
                        (Germany, France, USA, Japan)
The process of late industrialisation in France, Germany, the USA, and Japan refers to their rapid economic transformation during
the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, as they adopted and adapted industrial technologies pioneered by Britain. Through
state intervention, technological innovation, and the rise of large enterprises, these nations swiftly narrowed the industrial gap,
reshaping global economic and political dynamics.

       2.      “Latecomer” Industrial revolution in Japan involved certain factors that were markedly

6.1. American Industrialization
The industrialisation of America marked a transformative period when the nation shifted from an agrarian economy to one
driven by manufacturing, mechanisation, and technological innovation. This era not only accelerated economic growth
and urbanisation but also fundamentally changed the social and economic fabric of the United States.

6.1.1. Factors Responsible for the Industrialization of America

                      steel, accelerated the movement of goods, and encouraged mass production and economic specialisation.

6.2. Industrial Revolution in France
France industrialised later than Britain but remained one of the leading industrial economies of continental Europe with the process
beginning in the late eighteenth century and accelerating during the nineteenth century. The country’s industrialisation advanced
through the mechanisation of the textile sector and the expansion of heavy industries such as iron, steel, and coal, reaching its
peak during the rapid development under the Second Empire.

6.2.1. Factors Responsible for the Industrialisation of France

       Factor                                                           Description

 Entrepreneurship        Prominent families like the Schneiders (steel, rail) and Peugeots (tools, later automobiles) helped
 and Investment          expand industry with private capital and joint-stock ventures.

6.3. Industrial Revolution in Germany
Germany’s industrialisation began in the early nineteenth century with the creation of the Zollverein (customs union) in 1834,
which facilitated free trade among the German states and laid the foundation for economic growth. The unification of Germany
in 1871 under Otto von Bismarck marked a turning point, as unified policies and strategic initiatives accelerated the nation’s industrial
development.

6.3.1. Factors Responsible for the Industrialisation of Germany

        Factor                                                           Description
 Abundant Natural         Germany’s industrial surge was driven by rich coal reserves in the Ruhr Valley and iron ore

 with France              steel, coal, and armament production, to enhance military strength.

 Innovations
 Entrepreneurial          Visionaries like Friedrich Krupp and Werner von Siemens led industrial expansion, supported by

6.4. Industrial Revolution in Japan
The Industrial Revolution in Japan unfolded during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The Meiji Restoration of
1868 signaled the start of this transformation, with the new government launching a series of reforms called Bunmei Kaika (Civilisation
and Enlightenment) to modernise and westernise the nation.

Social                     Industrialisation involved a controlled transition, with the state managing change, unlike in the
Transformation             West, where it led to major social disruptions.

6.5. Impacts of the Industrial Revolution

                                                      Political Impacts

Expansion of             The rise of the industrial and professional middle class led to demands for political participation,
Democracy                resulting in reforms like the First Reform Act of 1832, which expanded political participation for

                        severe hardship.

 and Lower Costs        economies.

The late industrialisation of countries like Germany, the USA, Japan, and France marked a period of rapid economic
transformation in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. By adopting advanced technologies and implementing strategic
policies, these nations quickly bridged the industrial gap, significantly influencing global economic and political power
structures. As Karl Marx famously noted, “The Industrial Revolution has converted the modest workshop of the patriarchal
master into the great factory of the industrial capitalist.” This transformation not only reshaped economies but also redefined
social and political dynamics across the world.

7               Rise of Ideologies

Capitalism, socialism, and communism are influential political philosophies that define how societies structure ownership, resource
distribution, and the role of government. Understanding their fundamental differences, from private ownership and free markets
in capitalism to collective ownership and state planning in socialism and communism, is essential for analysing global socioeconomic
and political developments.
7.1. Capitalism
Capitalism is an economic system in which the means of production, trade, and industry are predominantly owned and controlled
by private individuals or businesses, with decisions guided by market forces of supply and demand. It emphasises private
property, profit motive, competition, and market-driven economic activity, making it the dominant model in the Western world,
which has evolved gradually since the end of feudalism.

7.1.1. Characteristics of Capitalism

         Aspect                                                         Description
 Private Property             Individuals and businesses have the right to own and control assets, including land, factories,
                              and capital goods.
 Objective                    The primary objective of economic activity is to earn profits, driving innovation and efficiency.
 Free Market & Price          Prices and production are determined by supply and demand with minimal government
 Mechanism                    interference; markets allocate resources efficiently.
 Competition                  Rivalry among businesses encourages better products, lower prices, and increased efficiency.
 Consumer Sovereignty         Consumers have the freedom to choose what to buy, influencing production decisions.
 Freedom of Enterprise        Individuals are free to start businesses, choose occupations, and make economic decisions.
 Limited Government           The government primarily protects property rights and maintains legal order, though modern
 Role                         capitalist states may regulate markets and provide welfare and public goods.
 Division of Labour           Specialisation of labour increases productivity and efficiency in production.
 Flexibility in the           Firms can hire and fire workers based on market needs, allowing labour to move where it is
 Labour Market                most valued, though often regulated by labour laws and contracts.
 Inheritance Rights           Property can be transferred to heirs, ensuring continuity of private ownership.
 International Trade          Low trade barriers promote the exchange of goods and services across borders.

7.1.2. Causes for the Growth of Capitalism

        Cause                                                         Description
 Industrial               y The advent of machines, factory systems, and inventions like the steam engine led to mass
 Revolution &               production, increased efficiency, and economic transformation.
 Technological            y Example: James Watt’s steam engine boosted coal and textile industries, making 18th-century
 Innovation                 Britain a pioneer of industrial capitalism.
 Accumulation of          y Colonial trade, banking expansion, rise of merchants and commercial profits contributed
 Capital & Surplus          significantly to capital accumulation.
 Wealth                   y Example: Profits from the British East India Company's trade with India and China, and banking
                            houses like the Rothschilds, fuelled industrial investments in 18th–19th century Europe.
 Urbanization             y Migration from rural to urban areas created a large industrial workforce and stimulated
 & Demographic              economic growth and consumer demand.
 Changes                  y Example: Between 1750 and 1850, cities like Manchester and Birmingham in Britain saw rapid
                            population growth due to rural-urban migration, supplying labor to factories.


 Supportive State         y Governments, especially in Britain, promoted free markets, minimal regulation, and policies
 Policies & Laissez-        favorable to industrial and commercial interests.
 faire                    y Example: The repeal of the Corn Laws in 1846 by the British Parliament removed trade
                            restrictions, supporting industrial capitalism and lowering food prices.
 Expanding                y Population growth, urbanisation, and access to abundant natural resources (like coal and iron)
 Markets & Access           and colonial markets fueled industrial expansion and competition.
 to Resources             y Example: Britain's access to coal and iron in Wales and northern England, and captive colonial
                            markets like India, provided both raw materials and outlets for finished goods.
Capitalism transformed the global economy by promoting private enterprise, innovation, and market-driven growth, becoming
the dominant economic system after the Industrial Revolution. At the same time, its inequalities and labour exploitation gave rise
to reform movements and alternative ideologies such as socialism.

7.2. Socialism
Socialism is a socio-economic and political ideology that advocates collective or social ownership of the means of
production and greater economic equality to ensure equitable distribution of resources and social justice. Emerging in the
19th century as a response to the inequalities of industrial capitalism, socialism aims to create a more just and equal society
by prioritising the welfare of all over individual profit.

7.2.1. Characteristics of Socialism

         Aspect                                                         Description
 Collective Ownership        Means of production such as land, factories, and resources are owned and managed collectively
                             by the state or community.
 Central Economic            Economic decisions regarding production, distribution, and investment are made by a central
 Planning                    authority or the government.
 Economic and Social         Focuses on reducing inequalities in wealth, income, and social status, promoting equal
 Equality                    opportunities for all.
 Lack of Competition         The state or community directs economic activity, minimising or eliminating competition found
                             in capitalist systems.
 Focus on Social             Ensures that basic needs like health, education, and employment are met for everyone,
 Welfare                     prioritising the welfare of society.
 State Control and           The government actively regulates and controls economic activities, including prices, wages, and
 Regulation                  resource allocation.
 Work and Wages              Distribution is generally linked to contribution while aiming to reduce inequality.
 Principle
 Restriction of Private      Private ownership in key sectors may be restricted or regulated for social benefit.
 Property

7.2.2. Causes for the Growth of Socialism
         Cause                                                         Explanation
 Industrialization &        y Rapid industrial growth led to poor working conditions, low wages, and urban poverty,
 Worker Exploitation          causing widespread discontent.
                            y Example: In 19th-century Britain, factory workers in cities like Manchester faced 14–16 hour
                              workdays and dangerous conditions, prompting calls for reform and workers’ rights.
 Economic Inequality        y The growing gap between wealthy industrialists and the working poor intensified demands
 & Class Conflict             for social and economic justice.
                            y Example: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels wrote The Communist Manifesto (1848) in response
                              to capitalist inequality seen during the Industrial Revolution in Europe.
 Growth of Labour           y Workers organised themselves to demand better wages, working hours, and conditions,
 Movements                    spreading socialist ideas.
                            y Example: The formation of trade unions and events like the 1871 Paris Commune showed early
                              working-class attempts at establishing socialist governance.


 Influence of Socialist     y New ideas promoting collective ownership and state intervention gained popularity as solutions
 Ideologies                   to social problems.
                            y Example: The Russian Revolution of 1917 was directly inspired by Marxist-Leninist socialism
                              and led to the establishment of the Soviet state under Bolshevik leadership.
 Urbanization &             y Expansion of cities brought challenges like unemployment, inadequate housing, and poor
 Social Issues                sanitation, highlighting the need for reform.
                            y Example: In early 20th-century Germany, socialist parties like the Social Democratic Party
                              (SPD) gained support in urban centers by advocating for housing, labor, and health reforms.
 Political                  y Limited rights and political freedoms encouraged people to seek alternative systems that
 Oppression                   promised greater democracy and equality.
                            y Example: In Tsarist Russia, repression under autocracy led to the growth of revolutionary
                              socialist movements like the Mensheviks and Bolsheviks.
 Inspiration                y Ideals of equality and collective good inspired efforts to build a fairer and more inclusive society.
 from Egalitarian           y Example: The French Revolution (1789), though not socialist, inspired later egalitarian movements
 Movements                    with its ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity – echoed in socialist thought.
Socialism has significantly shaped the evolution of modern political and economic systems by highlighting the need for social
justice, economic redistribution, and labour rights. Its principles continue to inform welfare policies and debates on balancing
economic growth with social equity.

7.3. Communism
Communism is a socio-political and economic ideology that aims to establish a classless, stateless, and moneyless society based on
the common (collective) ownership of the means of production, striving to eliminate economic inequality and social hierarchies.
Developed by 19th-century thinkers Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, communism envisions a system where private ownership
is replaced by collective ownership for the benefit of all.

7.3.1. Characteristics of Communism
       Key Characteristic                                                      Description
 Abolition of Private Property           Individual ownership of property is eliminated; private ownership of productive
                                         property is abolished in favour of collective ownership.
 End or limited inheritance              The concept of inheritance of private property is fundamentally challenged and often
                                         abolished.
 State Seizure of Property               Revolutionary communist regimes often nationalised property and confiscated the
                                         assets of opponents.
 Equal Work Distribution                 Communist systems emphasise collective labour and state-directed production.
 Universal Free Education                Free public schooling is provided for all children; child labour is prohibited.
 State Control of Finance                All financial resources and institutions are managed by the state.
 State Control of Communication          The government oversees all communication systems and transportation networks.
 and Transport
 State-Owned Industry                    Industries and major productive resources are state-owned and centrally managed.

7.3.2. Causes of Communism
        Cause                                                           Description
 Industrial               y Rapid industrialisation led to poor working conditions, urban poverty, and stark economic
 Revolution                 inequality, fuelling resentment against capitalism.
 & Capitalist             y Example: In 19th-century Britain and Germany, industrial workers faced exploitative conditions,
 Exploitation               which Marx and Engels observed firsthand, forming the empirical basis for their critique of
                            capitalism.
 Emergence and            y The rise of a large, exploited proletariat, along with the formation of trade unions and socialist
 Mobilization of the        parties, provided a strong base for communist ideas.
 Working Class            y Example: The formation of the German Social Democratic Party (SPD) and growing working-
                            class movements in Europe laid the groundwork for organised Marxist and communist movements.


 Influence of              y Early socialist thinkers and the writings of Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels offered a theoretical
 Socialist and               foundation and popularized the ideals of class struggle and collective ownership.
 Communist                 y Example: The Communist Manifesto in 1848 provided a rallying ideological framework that later
 Thinkers                    inspired revolutions such as the Russian Revolution of 1917.
 Impact of                 y The French Revolution’s emphasis on equality and the reaction against political repression inspired
 Revolutionary and           movements seeking radical social and economic change.
 Egalitarian Ideals        y Example: The slogan “Liberty, Equality, Fraternity” from the French Revolution (1789) inspired
                             19th-century radical thinkers and shaped the ideological environment that gave rise to communism.
Communism emerged as a radical alternative to capitalism, advocating collective ownership, class equality, and the end of
exploitation. Despite differing outcomes in practice, its ideas significantly shaped global politics, labour movements, and discussions
on social justice, state intervention, and economic equality.

7.4. Differences between Capitalism, Socialism, and Communism
       Aspect                    Capitalism                           Socialism                          Communism

                          century, associated with         popularised by utopian               Barmby (1840); formalised by
                          critics like Louis Blanc and     socialists                           Marx & Engels (1848)
                          Proudhon
 Economic                 Decentralised decision-          Combination of state planning        Central planning authority
 Coordination             making through market            + market mechanisms                  determines production and
                          signals                                                               distribution

 Economy                  innovation-driven                balancing efficiency and equity      and centrally directed
 Incentive                Driven by profit motive          Based on social welfare +            Based on collective goals, limited
 Structure                and market rewards               regulated incentives                 role of individual incentives

                          of religion                      social regulation                    religion may be discouraged/
                                                                                                suppressed

                          responsibility

Capitalism, socialism, and communism have profoundly shaped the modern world by offering distinct frameworks for ownership,
resource distribution, and the role of government. Their fundamental differences in private versus collective ownership, economic
equality, and state intervention have influenced the socio-economic and political development of nations, highlighting the ongoing
global debate over the best path to prosperity and social justice.

8              Russian Revolution

The Russian Revolution, particularly the October (Bolshevik) Revolution of 1917, was the world's first successful socialist
revolution. Led by the Bolsheviks under Vladimir Lenin and inspired by Marxist ideals, it sought to establish a socialist state
based on equality and workers' rule. The revolution overthrew the Provisional Government, ended centuries of Tsarist autocracy,
laid the foundation for the Soviet Union, and profoundly shaped global politics and the spread of communism throughout the
twentieth century.


8.1. Causes of Russian Revolution
       Causes                                                         Description
Agrarian Crisis and         Despite the abolition of serfdom in 1861, Russian peasants remained burdened by debt,
Peasant Discontent          poverty, and exploitation, leading to widespread rural unrest.
Backward                    Agriculture suffered due to lack of investment, outdated techniques, and the aristocracy’s
Agriculture                 reluctance to modernise, causing persistent rural stagnation.
Industrialization and       Late industrialisation subjected urban workers to harsh conditions, low wages, and limited
Worker Unrest               rights, fuelling strikes and demands for reforms.
Nationalities and           Russia’s vast empire included many ethnic groups whose aspirations for autonomy and cultural
Ethnic Tensions             recognition led to tensions and resistance.
Impact of World War         Russia’s involvement in WWI strained the economy and led to food shortages, inflation, and
I                           military defeats, deepening public dissatisfaction with the regime.
Russo-Japanese War          Russia's defeat humiliated the empire, exposed the Tsar’s inefficiency, and triggered the 1905
(1904-1905)                 revolution, a precursor to 1917.
Role of Intellectuals       Marxist thinkers and revolutionaries such as Lenin inspired masses through anti-Tsarist ideas
and Revolutionaries         and literature.
Nihilism and Radical        Nihilist philosophy and radical actions, including assassinations, inspired youth and challenged
Movements                   the legitimacy of the Tsarist state.
Influence of                Works by authors like Maxim Gorky, Dostoyevsky, and Tolstoy highlighted social injustice and
Literature                  inspired calls for change.
Leadership of               Leaders such as Lenin, Trotsky, and later Stalin provided vision, organisation, and strategy for
Revolutionaries             the revolutionary movement.

8.2. Major Events of the Russian Revolution
         Event                                                         Description
Growing Discontent            Sections of the nobility, middle classes, workers, and peasants increasingly opposed the Tsarist
(19th-early 20th century)     regime, setting the stage for revolution.
February Revolution           Strikes and food riots in Petrograd, fuelled by WWI hardships, led to Tsar Nicholas II’s
(1917)                        abdication. The Duma formed a Provisional Government, ending 300 years of Romanov rule.
Provisional                   y The Provisional Government, later led prominently by Aleksandr Kerensky, attempted
Government                      democratic reforms but lost support by continuing WWI, delaying the Constituent
                                Assembly, and failing to enact economic reforms.
                              y The growing influence of Soviets further eroded its authority.
Emergence of Lenin            y Lenin returned from exile in April 1917, demanding immediate withdrawal from WWI,
                                worker control, and Soviet-based governance.
                              y His radical ideas offered alternatives to the failing Provisional Government and gained
                                mass Bolshevik support.
Bolshevik (October)           y On Oct 24-25, the Bolsheviks (led by Lenin) overthrew the Provisional Government in
Revolution (1917)               Petrograd.
                              y Lenin established Soviet rule with the slogan “Peace, Land, and Bread".
                              y Russia exited WWI, and the world's first communist state was born.
Russian Civil War             y After the Bolshevik takeover, a civil war broke out between the Red Army (communists)
(1917–1923)                     and the White Army (monarchists, liberals, and foreign-backed forces).
                              y The Red Army emerged victorious, the Romanovs were executed in 1918, and the USSR
                                was officially established in 1922.

8.3. Significance of the Russian Revolution
   Aspect                                                          Description
Economic            y Ended centuries of monarchy and abolished private ownership of land, factories, and banks.
Impact              y Nationalised all major assets and implemented central planning for coordinated development.
                    y Abolished landlordism and redistributed land to peasants, promoting economic equality.

                    y Guaranteed free healthcare, education, unemployment allowance, and equal access to culture.

                    y Emphasised workers’ control and collective decision-making.

                    y Spread socialist ideas internationally, influencing revolutions in countries like China, Cuba, and

                    y Shifted the global geopolitical order, eventually contributing to the rise of the USSR as a major global

8.4. Impacts of the Russian Revolution on India
      Impact                                                       Description

 Motivation             mobilisation and revolutionary action could successfully overthrow autocratic regimes.

 Indian                 Subhash Chandra Bose were influenced by Soviet anti-imperialism and socialist principles.

                        mixed economic planning and policies such as the Five-Year Plans and a focus on heavy industry.
 Rise of              y The Russian Revolution directly inspired the establishment of the Communist Party of India.
 Communist            y Early Indian communists received training in the Soviet Union, and leaders such as M.N. Roy played
 Movement               an important role in the early communist movement in India.
 Radicalization       y Marxist influence and the growth of the communist movement led to a more radical Indian
 of National            National Movement, incorporating class struggle and workers’ rights and aligning India’s freedom
 Movement               struggle with global anti-imperialist efforts.
 Impact on            y The British perceived the spread of Bolshevik ideas as a threat, responding with measures like the
 British Policy         Montagu Declaration of 1917, which promised gradual self-government.
                      y They also became more repressive, viewing nationalist activities as “Bolshevik conspiracies".

The Russian Revolution marked a turning point in world history by ending centuries of autocratic rule and establishing the
world's first socialist state. Guided by the slogan "Peace, Bread, and Land"—Peace through immediate withdrawal from
World War I, Bread by addressing food shortages and improving the living conditions of workers, and Land through the
redistribution of land from landlords to peasants—the Bolsheviks consolidated popular support. The revolution inspired
socialist and anti-colonial movements worldwide and reshaped the global political landscape.

9               Crisis of Capitalism

The crisis of capitalism became evident in the early twentieth century when the Great Depression (1929-39) exposed the failures of
laissez-faire economic policies and unregulated markets, leading to economic collapse, mass unemployment, and global instability.
This crisis not only weakened faith in capitalist systems but also triggered political consequences, most notably the rise of Fascism
and Nazism as alternative responses to economic and social distress.

9.1. Great Depression (1929-1939)
The Great Depression was a severe global economic crisis that began with the Wall Street Crash of 1929 and lasted through the
1930s, profoundly impacting economies and societies worldwide. Originating in the United States, it led to widespread unemployment,
poverty, and a dramatic shift in economic policies and government intervention across nations.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    What policy instruments were deployed to contain the Great Economic Depression? (2013)

9.1.1. Causes of the Great Depression (1929–39)

        Cause                                                          Description
 Stock Market             The 1929 Wall Street Crash eroded wealth, confidence, and investment. Speculative bubbles and
 Crash                    margin buying caused stock prices to plummet, triggering widespread economic panic and decline.
 Bank Failures            Thousands of banks collapsed as panicked depositors withdrew savings. Without deposit insurance,
                          banks lacked liquidity, leading to a credit crunch that deepened the economic downturn.
 Overproduction           Industry and agriculture produced beyond effective purchasing demand, causing surpluses. Falling
                          prices led to layoffs and farm foreclosures, reducing income and exacerbating the economic slump
                          across sectors.
 Wealth Inequality        A vast wealth gap limited mass purchasing power. The elite held most income, reducing consumer
                          spending, which was critical to sustaining economic growth and stability.
 Reduced Consumer         Post-crash unemployment and fear cut household spending. Lower demand for goods and
 Spending                 services triggered layoffs and production cuts, creating a cycle of economic contraction.
 Weak Monetary            The Federal Reserve’s contractionary monetary policies and failure to stabilise banks worsened
 Policy                   the credit crisis, hindering economic recovery efforts.
 Global Trade             The Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act raised U.S. tariffs, prompting global retaliation. Trade plummeted,
 Collapse                 hurting export-reliant economies and intensifying the worldwide economic downturn.
 International            Post-World War I debts and reparations weakened Europe. The 1931 Creditanstalt bank failure
 Instability              spread global financial panic, intensifying the worldwide economic depression.

                          y In the US, millions lost their jobs, leading to widespread poverty and homelessness.

 Banking Systems            public confidence and mass withdrawal of funds, paralysing the financial system.

 of Welfare State        y In the US, Franklin D. Roosevelt’s New Deal brought large-scale public works, financial

       Response                                                        Explanation

 (Public Spending)          roads, dams, and WPA), aimed at boosting employment and consumer demand.

 Reforms                    Federal Reserve) loosened monetary policy post-1933 to encourage borrowing and investment.

                            economic recovery. Countries that exited early recovered faster.

The Great Depression not only devastated economies but also deeply affected the social fabric, as poverty and unemployment led
to widespread despair and unrest. As Studs Terkel observed, “What I remember most of those times is that poverty creates
desperation, and desperation creates violence,” highlighting the profound human consequences of economic crises.
9.2. Rise of Fascism and Nazism
The rise of Fascism and Nazism in early 20th-century Europe represents the emergence of far-right authoritarian ideologies,
rooted in ultra-nationalism, anti-democratic tendencies, and mass mobilisation. These regimes arose in the backdrop of post-
World War I crises, including the Treaty of Versailles, economic instability, and the failure of liberal democracies, which created
conditions for dictatorial leadership and popular discontent. They led to the establishment of totalitarian states, characterised
by suppression of dissent, propaganda, militarism, and expansionism, ultimately culminating in the outbreak of the Second
World War.


9.2.1. Fascism
Fascism, defined by extreme right-wing authoritarian nationalism, dictatorial rule, and the violent repression of opposition,
rose to prominence in Europe during the early twentieth century. This ideology imposed strict government control over society
and the economy, emphasising absolute state authority and collective unity over personal liberties.

9.2.1.1. Reasons for the Rise of Fascism in Italy

         Reason                                                          Description
 Discontent with              Italy emerged victorious in WWI but did not receive all the promised territorial rewards (such
 Versailles Treaty            as the Dalmatian Coast and Fiume), leading to a sense of national betrayal.
 Post-war Strikes             Known as the "Biennio Rosso", a wave of strikes, factory occupations, and peasant uprisings
 (1919–1920)                  created social chaos and fear of communist revolution.
 Economic Crisis after        The aftermath of World War I left Italy’s economy in turmoil, with rising unemployment,
 the War                      particularly among returning soldiers.
 Weakness of                  The proportional representation system resulted in fragmented parliaments and frequent
 Parliamentary System         government changes, causing instability.
 Political and Social         Widespread dissatisfaction with existing leadership created a demand for a strong, decisive
 Disillusionment              figure, paving the way for Mussolini.
 Charismatic                  Mussolini’s promises of order, stability, and protection of property appealed to a population
 Leadership 6                 weary of chaos and uncertainty.
 Disunity Among               Socialists, communists, and liberals were divided, failed to form a united front against fascists,
 Opposition                   and underestimated Mussolini’s threat.
 Rise of Hyper                Italian Fascism initially focused more on nationalism and anti-socialism than racial ideology,
 Nationalism                  though anti-Semitic policies intensified in the late 1930s to align with Nazi Germany.

                           support.

 Violence

9.2.2. Nazism
Nazism, an extreme right-wing ideology rooted in German nationalism and racial supremacy, emerged in early 20th-century
Germany under Adolf Hitler’s leadership. Combining totalitarian control, aggressive expansionism, and systematic persecution
of minorities, Nazism led to the establishment of a dictatorial regime and played a central role in the outbreak of World War II.

Weak Weimar               The Democratic Weimar Republic was plagued by political instability, coalition breakdowns, and an
Leadership                inability to address national crises effectively.
Impact of the Great       The global economic downturn of 1929 severely affected Germany, worsening unemployment and
Depression                fuelling public anger.

Communism                 Nazis as a bulwark against it.
Hitler’s Leadership       Hitler’s powerful oratory, charisma, and nationalist rhetoric attracted mass support and positioned
                          him as a strong leader.

                          ambitions.
Social                    Deep social divisions allowed the Nazis to scapegoat Jews and other minorities, uniting others
Fragmentation             through blame and exclusion.

Service

Rhineland                    violating international agreements like Treaty of Versailles and the Locarno Pact.
Annexation of Austria        In 1938, Germany forcibly incorporated Austria into the Reich, breaching the Treaty of
                             Versailles.

Sudetenland                  Czechoslovakia in 1939.
Expansionist Policies        Hitler pursued the creation of a “Greater Germany,” signing a nonaggression pact with the

The rise of Fascism and Nazism in early 20th-century Europe was fuelled by economic hardship, political instability, and public
disillusionment, leading to the establishment of authoritarian regimes that dismantled democratic institutions and suppressed
dissent. As Adolf Hitler aptly stated, “The one means that wins the easiest victory over reason: terror and force," reflecting how
these ideologies relied on fear and violence to consolidate power, ultimately shaping the course of world history.

chapters.t: World Wars and Redrawing of Boundaries
chapters.text:
10                  World Wars and Redrawing
                         of Boundaries
The two World Wars were unprecedented global conflicts in the 20th century, involving major powers across continents and resulting
in massive destruction and loss of life. Their profound political, economic, and social consequences reshaped international relations
and set the stage for the modern world order.
                                                  UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    How far is it correct to say that the First World War was fought essentially for the
            preservation of balance of power? (2024)
      2.    “There arose a serious challenge to the Democratic State System between the two World
            Wars.” Evaluate the statement. (2021)
      3.    To what extent can Germany be held responsible for causing the two World Wars?
            Discuss critically (2015)

10.1. World War I
The First World War (1914-1918), also known as the Great War, was a global conflict that engulfed most of Europe, along with
parts of Asia, Africa, and the Middle East, primarily involving the Allied Powers and the Central Powers . It emerged from deep-
rooted tensions such as militarism, alliance systems, imperial rivalries, and aggressive nationalism and was immediately triggered
by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.

The war caused unprecedented destruction and mass casualties, led to the collapse of major empires, and marked a decisive
turning point in 20th-century geopolitics, ultimately laying the foundation for future global conflicts, including the Second
World War.


                                        The two rival military alliances of World War I
Note: Although Italy was a member of the Triple Alliance, it remained neutral in 1914, arguing that the alliance was
  defensive and Austria-Hungary had declared war first. After signing the secret Treaty of London (1915), which
  promised territorial gains, Italy joined the Triple Entente and declared war on Austria-Hungary in May 1915.


10.1.1 Causes of World War I

   Cause                                                          Description
Imperial          Competition among established powers (Britain, France and Russia) and emerging ones (Germany,
Rivalries         Austria-Hungary, Italy, Japan) for colonies, economic dominance and global influence heightened
                  tensions and disrupted the European balance of power.
Colonial          The pursuit of overseas colonies for resources and markets, driven by the Industrial Revolution, led to
Expansion         frequent clashes and fuelled nationalist rivalries, especially as Germany sought to expand its colonial
                  holdings.
Nationalism       Rising nationalist sentiments, particularly among ethnic groups within multi-ethnic empires like Austria-
                  Hungary and the Ottoman Empire, led to demands for self-determination and contributed to
                  instability, especially in the Balkans.
Decline of        Weakening of the Ottoman Empire (“sick man of Europe”) and Austria-Hungary created a power
Empires           vacuum in the Balkans, intensifying nationalist movements and regional conflicts such as the Balkan Wars
                  of 1912-1913.
Role of           Germany’s aggressive colonial ambitions, naval arms race with Britain, and blank cheque’ support to
Germany           Austria-Hungary after the Sarajevo assassination escalated the crisis, while misjudgements regarding
                  Russia's and France’s responses led to wider conflict.
Mutual            The entanglement of alliances—the Triple Entente (Britain, France, Russia) and the Triple Alliance
Defence           (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy) - meant that a local conflict rapidly involved all major powers as
Alliances         they honoured treaty commitments to their allies.
Immediate         The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria by a Bosnian Serb nationalist on June 28,
Trigger           1914, set off a chain reaction of declarations of war, transforming a regional dispute into a global
                  conflict.

10.1.3. Phases of World War I
     Phase                                                      Description
Beginning            y World War I began on July 28, 1914, when Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia following the
(July 1914)            assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, setting off a chain reaction among allied nations.
Phase 1: Opening     y The initial stage featured Germany attempting a rapid offensive based on the Schlieffen Plan,
Campaigns (1914)       aiming for a rapid victory over France before turning to Russia.

                       Western Front.
Phase 2: Stalemate   y This period was marked by prolonged trench warfare and massive casualties in battles such as
and Attrition          Verdun, the Somme, and Gallipoli.
(1915–1917)          y New powers like the Ottoman Empire and Italy joined the conflict, while Russia withdrew after the

1918)                  collapse of Germany and its allies.

10.1.4. Consequences of the World War I
     Aspect                                                     Description
Economic             y The war imposed enormous financial burdens on all participants, with countries like Germany
Consequences           and Britain spending around 60% of their GDP on the conflict.

                       money, which led to severe post-war inflation.
Political            y Four major empires, the Russian, German, Austro-Hungarian, and Ottoman collapsed, resulting
Consequences           in the emergence of new nation-states such as Poland, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, Finland,
                       Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania.
                     y The map of Europe and the Middle East was redrawn, with Britain and France gaining mandates
                       over former Ottoman territories like Palestine, Syria, Lebanon, and Iraq.
                     y The war weakened European powers, paving the way for the rise of the United States and the
                       Soviet Union as global powers.
                     y The principle of self-determination gained prominence but also led to new ethnic tensions and
                       future conflicts.
Social               y The war caused massive casualties, resulting in millions of deaths, injuries, widows, and orphans,
Consequences           and a significant decline in birth rates.
                     y Many civilians became refugees, losing their homes and livelihoods.
                     y Women entered the workforce in large numbers, and post-war, many countries expanded
                       women’s rights, including suffrage.
                     y The traditional dominance of the upper classes was challenged as the middle and lower classes
                       demanded greater participation in governance.
International        y The global power structure shifted, with the United States emerging as a leading world power
Consequences           and the Soviet Union inspiring global communist movements.
                     y The ideological divide between Capitalism and Communism set the stage for the Cold War.
                     y Social unrest and fear of communism contributed to the rise of right-wing extremism and fascist
                       movements in several countries.
                     y The League of Nations was established to promote international peace and resolve disputes,
                       serving as a precursor to the United Nations, though its effectiveness was limited.

Military            y As a British colony, India supplied over a million soldiers and vast resources to the Allied war
Contribution          effort.
                    y Indian troops fought in major campaigns such as Gallipoli (1915), Mesopotamia (1914–1918), and

10.1.7. First World War as a Struggle for Balance of Power
