                             y Example: The Triple Entente and Triple Alliance existed to prevent hegemony.

10.2. World War II
The Second World War (1939–1945) was the largest and deadliest war in history, fought between the Axis Powers (Germany,
Italy, and Japan) and the Allied Powers (Britain, USA, USSR, France, China). It emerged from a combination of unresolved
tensions after World War I (Treaty of Versailles), aggressive expansionism, economic instability, and the rise of Fascist regimes.
The war started with the invasion of Poland and soon spread across the world, becoming a total war with heavy loss of life and
destruction. It led to major changes in global politics, including the rise of the United States and the Soviet Union as superpowers
and the emergence of the Cold War.


10.2.1. Causes of World War II
      Cause                                                        Description
Treaty of Versailles   y The harsh terms imposed on Germany after World War I—including territorial losses, military
                         restrictions, and heavy reparations—fueled resentment and paved the way for the rise of Adolf
                         Hitler and the Nazi Party, who promised to restore Germany’s power and prestige.
National               y The creation of new nation-states after WWI left large ethnic minorities, especially Germans in
Minorities Issue         Poland and Czechoslovakia.
                       y Hitler exploited the grievances of German minorities to justify aggression in these regions.
Economic               y The Great Depression led to severe economic hardship worldwide, increasing unemployment
Depression               and poverty.
(1929)                 y Economic instability contributed to the rise of authoritarian regimes in Germany, Italy, and
                         Japan, as people turned to leaders promising recovery and national revival.
Failure of League      y The League of Nations, established to maintain peace, proved ineffective due to weak
of Nations               enforcement mechanisms and lack of commitment from major powers.
                       y It failed to prevent acts of aggression, such as Italy’s invasion of Ethiopia and Japan’s
                         occupation of Manchuria.
Aggression of Axis     y Japan invaded Manchuria (1931), later expanding into China; Italy invaded Ethiopia (1935);
Powers                   and Germany, under Hitler, remilitarised the Rhineland, annexed Austria, and occupied
                         Czechoslovakia.
                       y These aggressive actions went largely unchecked by the international community.
Axis Treaties          y Germany and Italy formed the Rome-Berlin Axis (1936) and later signed the Pact of Steel (1939).
                       y Japan joined this alliance with the Tripartite Pact (1940), creating a powerful coalition of Axis
                         Powers.
Failure of             y Britain and France, seeking to avoid another war, pursued a policy of appeasement, allowing
Appeasement              Hitler to annex territories such as the Sudetenland (Munich Conference, 1938).
                       y This emboldened Hitler, who broke agreements and continued his expansionist policies.
Immediate Cause:       y On September 1, 1939, Germany invaded Poland. Britain and France declared war on Germany,
Invasion of Poland       marking the official start of World War II.

10.2.2. Phases of World War II

      Phase                                                        Description
Outbreak of War        y Germany invaded Poland on September 1, 1939, prompting Britain and France to declare war
(1939)                   on Germany.
                       y The Soviet Union also invaded Poland from the east. Italy and the United States remained neutral
                         initially.
Early German           y Germany used “Blitzkrieg” tactics to quickly conquer Poland, Norway, Denmark, Belgium,
Victories (1939–41)      Holland, Luxembourg, and France.
                       y Paris fell in June 1940. Operation Sea-Lion, the planned invasion of Britain, failed after the
                         Luftwaffe was repelled by the Royal Air Force.
                       y Germany then turned its attention to Eastern Europe and the Soviet Union.
Operation              y On June 22, 1941, Germany launched a massive invasion of the Soviet Union.
Barbarossa (1941)      y Despite early advances, German advances stalled due to Soviet resistance, logistical difficulties,
                         and harsh winter conditions, particularly around Moscow and Leningrad.
Atlantic Charter &     y In August 1941, Roosevelt and Churchill issued the Atlantic Charter, outlining post-war goals.
United Nations         y On January 1, 1942, the Declaration of the United Nations was signed by the Allied powers,
(1941–42)                formalising their alliance against the Axis.
Entry of the United    y The U.S. entered the war after Japan’s attack on Pearl Harbour. (December 7, 1941).
States (1941)          y Italy and Germany declared war on the U.S., making the conflict truly global.
Turning Point:         y The German advance into the Soviet Union was decisively halted at Stalingrad, where the
Battle of Stalingrad     German 6th Army was encircled and forced to surrender in early 1943.
(1942–43)              y This marked the beginning of a major Soviet counteroffensive.


Defeat of Italy        y Allied forces invaded Sicily and mainland Italy. Italy surrendered in September 1943, and
(1943)                   Mussolini’s Fascist government collapsed, though he later headed a German-backed regime in
                         northern Italy.
Yalta Conference       y Allied leaders (Stalin, Churchill, Roosevelt) met to plan the final assault on Germany and post-war
(Feb 1945)               arrangements, including the division of Germany and the prosecution of war criminals.
Defeat of Germany      y Allied forces advanced from the west and east, surrounding Berlin. Hitler committed suicide on
(May 1945)               April 30, 1945.
                       y Germany surrendered unconditionally in early May, 1945, ending the war in Europe.
Potsdam Conference     y The Allied leaders met to finalise post-war plans for Germany, including demilitarisation,
(July–Aug 1945)          denazification, and the prosecution of war criminals (Nuremberg Trials).
Defeat of Japan        y After capturing much of Southeast Asia, Japan refused to surrender. The U.S. dropped atomic bombs
(Aug–Sep 1945)           on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9), and the Soviet Union declared war on Japan.
                       y Japan surrendered on September 2, 1945, officially ending World War II.

10.2.3. Consequences of the Second World War
    Aspect                                                          Description
Devastation and     y The war left much of Europe, Asia, and parts of Africa in ruins, with an unprecedented number of
Loss                  military and civilian casualties.
                    y The atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki caused total destruction in those cities.
Shift in Global     y The influence of old imperial powers like Britain and France declined.
Order               y The United States emerged as the leader of the democratic world, while the Soviet Union
                      established dominance in Eastern Europe, creating a new bipolar world order.
Economic            y By 1945, many countries faced severe economic challenges, including inflation, debt, and depleted
Hardships             reserves.
                    y The U.S. responded with the Marshall Plan through the Economic Cooperation Act of 1948,
                      providing financial aid to help rebuild Europe’s economies.
Nuclear Arms        y The use of atomic bombs marked the start of the nuclear age.
Race                y Efforts to control nuclear technology quickly failed, sparking a global race among nations to develop
                      nuclear weapons.
Beginning of the    y The defeat of the Axis powers led to ideological and political rivalry between the United States and
Cold War              the Soviet Union, resulting in the onset of the Cold War—a period of intense geopolitical tension and
                      competition.

10.2.4. Role of India in World War II
    Aspect                                                          Description
Largest              y The British Indian Army grew to 2.5 million soldiers, making it the largest volunteer force in the world.
Volunteer Army       y Around 89,000 Indian soldiers lost their lives fighting for the British Empire during the war.
Participation on     y Indian troops served in key theatres across Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia.
Major Fronts         y They played a vital role in battles such as the liberation of Italy at Monte Cassino.
Major Supplier       y India became a crucial supply base for the Allied campaign against Japan in Southeast Asia.
for the War          y Its factories provided textiles and essential war materials to Allied nations.
Impact on India      y The war accelerated political changes in India. Subhas Chandra Bose formed the Indian National Army
                       (INA) from Indian soldiers and POWs in Southeast Asia to fight British rule.
                     y The British plan to try INA members for treason sparked a surge of nationalism.
                     y The war also led to increased taxes, inflation, corruption, and the devastating Bengal famine of 1943.

10.2.5. Factors Behind the Decline of the Democratic State System during World Wars

       Factor                                                         Description
Economic Downturns       y The Great Depression (1929–1933) severely damaged the world economy, resulting in mass
and Democratic             unemployment, widespread poverty, and social unrest.
Ineffectiveness          y Democratic governments struggled to manage these crises, leading to public disillusionment and
                           the rise of extremist movements.


 Rise of Authoritarian      y Economic hardship and weak democratic institutions paved the way for authoritarian leaders.
 Regimes                    y Example: Mussolini established a fascist dictatorship in Italy (1922); Hitler and the Nazi Party
                              seized power in Germany (1933), promoting aggressive nationalism; Stalin consolidated his rule
                              in the Soviet Union, creating a repressive communist state.
 Ineffectiveness of the     y The League of Nations, created to preserve peace and promote diplomacy, lacked the authority
 League of Nations            and means to enforce its resolutions.
                            y Its credibility was further weakened by the absence or inconsistent participation of major
                              powers, especially the United States, and by member states’ unwillingness to commit to
                              collective security.
 Appeasement                y Western democracies, particularly Britain and France, pursued a policy of appeasement, allowing
 Strategy                     authoritarian regimes to expand without resistance.
                            y Example: The Munich Agreement (1938) permitted Nazi Germany to annex parts of
                              Czechoslovakia, demonstrating the failure to deter aggression and safeguard democracy.
The World Wars not only redrew political boundaries but also left deep scars on humanity, economies, and societies across the globe.
As Neville Chamberlain aptly stated, “In war, whichever side may call itself the victor, there are no winners, but all are losers,”
underscoring the universal tragedy of such conflicts.

11                 Colonialism and its Impacts
Colonialism in Africa and Asia during the 19th and 20th centuries was marked by the domination and exploitation of these regions
by European powers, fundamentally reshaping their political, economic, and social structures. Driven by the quest for resources,
markets, and strategic advantage, colonial rule disrupted traditional societies and set the stage for later independence movements
and decolonisation.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    Africa was chopped into states artificially created by accident of European competition.
            Analyse. (2013)


11.1. Causes of Colonialism in Africa and Asia
       Cause                                  Details                                               Examples
 Resource                European industries needed raw materials like           Indian cotton fuelled British textile mills;
 Extraction              cotton, minerals, and spices, pushing colonies to       African gold and diamonds were mined for
                         supply them cheaply.                                    European markets.
 Trade Routes and        Controlling key trade routes and new markets            British control over key ports such as Bombay
 Markets                 ensured economic dominance for European                 and Singapore strengthened Indian Ocean trade.
                         goods.
 Imperial Rivalry        European nations competed to expand empires,            Britain and France raced to claim African
                         boosting global influence and prestige.                 territories; Germany sought colonies in East
                                                                                 Africa.
 Strategic Control       Colonies served as military and naval bases to          British India was a base for Asian operations;
                         protect European interests in global conflicts.         French Indochina secured Southeast Asian
                                                                                 influence.
 Post-Industrial         Industrial growth increased demand for raw              Belgian Congo supplied rubber for European
 Revolution              materials and labour, making colonies essential.        industries; Indian jute fed British factories.
 Demand

                         "civilise" non-Western societies, spreading             promotion of English education in India.
                         Christianity and Western values.

        Legacy                                      Details                                              Examples

                            raw material exports, hindering economic growth.

                            and marginalised groups.                                     African urban elites vs rural poor.

                            institutions.
Colonialism in Africa and Asia resulted in profound political, economic, and social transformations, often marked by exploitation,
disruption of traditional structures, and the imposition of artificial borders. While it paved the way for later independence movements,
the legacy of colonial rule continues to shape the challenges and inequalities faced by these regions today.


11.2. Indentured Labour and Diaspora
Indentured labour involved contractual agreements binding Indian workers to 3–5 years of service in colonial plantations or
infrastructure projects under often harsh conditions, in exchange for passage, wages, and basic provisions. This system emerged
after the 1833 abolition of slavery in British colonies to address labour shortages and operated across British, French, and Dutch
colonies. While the system was officially banned in 1917, those already indentured had to complete contracts, so operations continued
into the early 1920s.

12                 Decolonization and Emergence
                        of New Nations
Decolonisation refers to the process by which Asian and African colonies gained independence from European powers, primarily
after 1945, through negotiations, struggles, or revolutions. Weakened European powers after World War II, rising nationalism, and
global anti-colonial sentiment accelerated independence movements across British, French, Dutch, and Portuguese colonies in Asia
and Africa.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.    What problems were germane to the decolonisation process in the Malay Peninsula? (2017)
      2.    The anti-colonial struggles in West Africa were led by the new elite of Western-educated
            Africans. Examine. (2016)

12.1. Causes of Decolonization in Asia and Africa
      Cause                                                         Explanation
 Impact of           y Both WWI and WWII weakened European colonial powers economically and militarily, reducing
 World Wars            their capacity to hold onto colonies.
                     y Example: After WWII, Britain and France faced enormous war debts and were unable to suppress
                       mass nationalist movements in India, Indonesia, and Algeria.
 Rise of             y Educated elites and grassroots activists organised resistance movements demanding self-rule.
 Nationalist         y Example: The Indian National Congress under Gandhi led a successful civil disobedience movement,
 Movements             inspiring similar struggles in Indonesia (Sukarno), Ghana (Nkrumah), and Kenya (Kenyatta).
 Weakening of        y The ideological basis for colonialism collapsed post-WWII, with principles of freedom and equality
 Colonial              gaining ground.
 Legitimacy          y Example: The Atlantic Charter’s (1941) emphasis on self-determination encouraged anti-colonial
                       leaders in Vietnam, India, and Nigeria to question continued foreign domination.
 Formation of        y The UN provided diplomatic backing to newly independent countries and gradually became an
 the United            important platform for anti-colonial movements.
 Nations (1945)      y Example: The creation of the UN Special Committee on Decolonisation empowered colonies to raise
                       their voice internationally, leading to formal independence in many cases.
 Mass                y In many regions, peaceful protests evolved into armed liberation movements when met with colonial
 Mobilisation          repression.
 and Armed           y Example: Algerian independence (1954–62) came after a violent war against France; Kenya’s Mau
 Struggles             Mau rebellion (1952–60) pushed Britain toward negotiations.
 International       y Global solidarity efforts accelerated the push for decolonization.
 Attention           y Example: The Bandung Conference (1955) united newly independent Asian and African nations to
                       support anti-colonial struggles globally and gave rise to the Non-Aligned Movement.

12.1.1. Role of Western-Educated Elites in Anti-Colonial Struggles in West Africa
        Aspect                                                       Explanation
 Awakening Political       y Western education exposed African elites to self rule, enlightenment ideals of liberty, equality,
 Consciousness               and justice, fuelling anti-colonial sentiment.
                           y Example: In Nigeria, Nnamdi Azikiwe emerged as a key nationalist leader and founder of the
                             National Council of Nigeria and the Cameroons (NCNC).
 Articulation through      y Many elites used newspapers, journals, and pamphlets to spread nationalist ideas and challenge
 Media and Press             colonial narratives.
 Negotiation and           y They negotiated with colonial governments for incremental political reforms and constitutional
 Constitutional              changes.
 Reforms                   y Example: Ghana's 1951 constitution was a result of negotiations led by educated elites like
                             Nkrumah, who transitioned from prison to Prime Minister within two years.
 Pan-Africanism            y They participated in pan-African forums to build solidarity and demand global support for
                             decolonization.
                           y Example: Manchester Pan-African Congress (1945), calling for immediate African independence.


Bridging Traditional     y While drawing from Western education, many leaders retained deep links to local cultures and
and Modern Spheres         traditions, easing the transition.
                         y Example: Nkrumah invoked both modern socialism and African traditional symbols to unify the
                           Gold Coast’s diverse population.
Role in Post-            y These elites transitioned into leadership roles in newly independent states, shaping their
Independence               constitutional and administrative systems.
Nation Building          y Example: After independence, Nkrumah became Ghana’s first Prime Minister, setting the tone for
                           African socialism and pan-Africanism.

12.1.2. Problems Germane to Decolonization in the Malay Peninsula

   Problem                                              Explanation with Examples
Ethnic              y The Malay Peninsula had a highly plural society comprising Malays, Chinese, Indians, and others,
Tensions              complicating nation-building and political representation.
                    y Example: The post-war period saw tensions between the Malays (indigenous majority) and Chinese
                      (economically dominant minority).
Communist           y The Malayan Communist Party, largely supported by Chinese communities, launched an armed
Insurgency            rebellion against British rule. The insurgency delayed independence and led to prolonged military
                      action.
                    y Example: The British imposed emergency rule in 1948 and fought guerrillas until 1960, making
                      Malaya's decolonisation unique for its armed conflict.
Strategic           y Britain was reluctant to leave due to the economic and military value of Malaya-its rubber and tin
Interests             resources and naval bases like Singapore.
                    y Example: British military bases in Singapore were seen as vital during the Cold War, influencing
                      Britain's cautious and protracted decolonization approach.
Formation of a      y The challenge of creating a shared Malayan national identity across diverse ethnic, religious, and
Unified Political     linguistic lines was central.
Identity            y Example: The formation of the United Malays National Organization (UMNO) was a strategic effort
                      to balance communal interests before independence in 1957.
Colonial            y The export-oriented economy dominated by British capital and foreign ownership made economic
Economic              restructuring difficult post-independence.
Structures          y Example: British firms controlled plantations, mines, and trade, leaving the local population
                      economically dependent and underdeveloped despite formal political independence.
Delayed Path to     y Compared to India or Indonesia, Malaya's independence was delayed due to internal instability,
Independence          territorial disputes and British caution.
                    y Example: Independence was only achieved in 1957 after nearly a decade of constitutional
                      negotiations, state-of-emergency governance, and efforts to suppress radical opposition.

12.1.3. Processes of Decolonization in Asia and Africa

    Process                                 Explanation                                           Examples

Decolonisation in Asia and Africa marked the end of colonial rule and the rise of independent nation-states, fundamentally reshaping
global politics. As Martin Luther King Jr aptly stated, “Freedom is never voluntarily given by the oppressor; it must be demanded
by the oppressed,” reflecting the determined struggles that defined these independence movements.

12.2. Arab Nationalism and West Asia - Suez Crisis
Arab nationalism (Pan-Arabism) emerged in the late 19th century as a movement for the unity of Arab peoples in the Middle
East and North Africa, based on a shared language, culture, and history. It gained momentum with the decline of the Ottoman
Empire and after World War I, seeking to end Western influence and establish political unity among Arab states. The 1948 Arab-
Israeli War marked a turning point, radicalising the movement by exposing Arab military and political weaknesses and strengthening
demands for unity and anti-imperialism. The Suez Crisis (1956) further reinforced Arab nationalism, symbolising resistance to
European imperialism and reshaping regional and global power dynamics.

13                 Cold War and Bipolar World

The Cold War (1945–1991) was a prolonged period of geopolitical tension and ideological rivalry between the United States-led
capitalist bloc and the Soviet Union-led communist bloc, which deeply influenced global politics and alliances. Characterised by
an arms race, proxy wars, and intense diplomatic standoffs, the Cold War shaped the course of the twentieth century without direct
military conflict between the two superpowers.

 Security Concerns          withdraw from these occupied territories heightened Western fears of Soviet expansionism and
                            global communist spread.

 and Truman                 spread of communism. It was implemented through measures such as the Truman Doctrine,

The Cold War fundamentally shaped global politics through decades of ideological rivalry, military alliances, and proxy conflicts
between the US and the USSR. Its conclusion, marked by the fall of the Berlin Wall and the dissolution of the Soviet Union in
1991, ended the bipolar world order and ushered in a new era of international relations.

14                 Global Institutions

Global institutions emerged to ensure peace, security, and cooperation, especially after World Wars I and II. The League of
Nations, though pioneering, failed due to structural weaknesses. Post-World War II, the United Nations was established, becoming
central in conflict resolution, development, and human rights. Despite successes, challenges persist-like power asymmetry,
frequent use of veto powers, and limited enforcement. Calls for reforms continue to make these institutions more inclusive and
effective in addressing complex global issues such as climate change, pandemics, and armed conflicts.

14.1. League of Nations
The League of Nations was established on January 10, 1920, under the Treaty of Versailles following World War I, inspired by
US President Woodrow Wilson’s Fourteen Points vision and post-war diplomacy among the Allied powers. It consisted of three
main organs: the Assembly, where all member states had equal voting rights; the Council, made up of permanent and elected
members; and the Secretariat, which handled administrative functions.

                           promote genuine self-rule.

                           Poland, the Mosul Dispute (1924) between Britain (Iraq) and Turkey, and the Greek–Bulgarian
                           Crisis (1925). However, these disputes were relatively minor and were often settled in favour of the
                           dominant powers.

 Unanimity)

 Authority                 could overrule decisions, as seen during the Corfu Incident (1923), reducing its credibility in
                           international affairs.

 Failure                   Treaty of Versailles, other major powers refused to reduce their armaments. The World
                           Disarmament Conference (1932–34) also ended without agreement.
 Weak Collective           The system of collective security remained weak because member states were not legally required
 Security                  to provide military assistance against aggressors. Proposed reforms, such as the Geneva Protocol
                           (1924), were never adopted.

 Aggression                  invasion of Abyssinia (1935), and Germany's expansion under Hitler. Economic sanctions were
                             weak, delayed, and inconsistently enforced, failing to deter aggressor states.
 Impact of the Great         The Great Depression weakened international cooperation, encouraged economic nationalism,
 Depression                  protectionism, and the rise of authoritarian regimes, leading countries to prioritise national
                             interests over collective security, further reducing its effectiveness.

The League of Nations laid the institutional and normative foundations of modern global governance, collective security, and
multilateral diplomacy. Many of its principles, institutions, and functions were later adopted by the United Nations. Although
officially dissolved in 1946, it marked the first global effort to promote international peace, collective security, and international
cooperation, leaving a lasting legacy for the post-war international order.

14.2. United Nations
The United Nations was established in 1945 after the Second World War as the successor to the League of Nations, with the
objective of overcoming the limitations of the League’s failed peacekeeping efforts. Committed to maintaining international peace
and security, promoting cooperation among nations, and addressing global challenges collectively, the UN inherited many of the
League’s goals, staff, and institutional assets. To effectively fulfil its objectives, the organisation was structured around key organs,
each entrusted with distinct functions in global governance and international cooperation.

Global institutions have evolved from early treaties and conferences, such as the Congress of Vienna, into modern multilateral
organisations like the United Nations, shaping international cooperation and governance. Despite their achievements in promoting
peace, development, and collective action, these institutions continue to face challenges in adapting to shifting global dynamics
and addressing emerging issues.
